source.title: Chemical Spill Contaminates Public Water Supply in Charleston, West Virginia

event.top_event: Loss of Containment from Aboveground Storage Tank
event.incident_type: Chemical spill to environment and drinking water contamination
actions_taken: WVDEP inspectors responded to odor complaints and discovered leak
Freedom deployed vacuum trucks to recover spilled material
WVAW issued Do Not Use order for 93,000 customer accounts
FEMA, West Virginia National Guard, and state agencies provided emergency water supply
Water treatment plant operated with granular activated carbon and powder activated carbon
Residents advised to restrict water use for drinking, cooking, and bathing
Tanks 395, 396, and 397 contents transferred to Baker storage containers
event.summary: On January 9, 2014, approximately 11,000 gallons of a mixture containing Crude MCHM and PPH, stripped leaked from tank 396 at Freedom Industries facility in Charleston, West Virginia. The leak resulted from pitting corrosion that created two holes (0.75 and 0.4 inches diameter) in the tank floor. The chemical mixture escaped through failed secondary containment and an underground culvert, flowing into the Elk River approximately 1.5 miles upstream of the West Virginia American Water treatment facility intake. The contamination resulted in a Do Not Use order affecting approximately 300,000 residents across nine counties. Over 300 people sought medical treatment for symptoms including nausea, rashes, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. The incident exposed significant gaps in tank inspection, secondary containment maintenance, leak detection systems, source water protection, and public health communication.
recommendations: AST owners and operators should establish regular inspection programs and routinely monitor tanks and secondary containment to verify tank integrity
AST owners and operators should coordinate with nearby water utilities and emergency response organizations to ensure chemical information is immediately available
AST owners and operators should ensure spill prevention and protection plans are updated and implemented
States should take immediate action to protect source waters through increased inspections and enforcement at chemical storage facilities near water sources
States should establish Source Water Assessment Programs that mandate source water protection planning by water utilities
Water utilities should engage with Local Emergency Planning Committees to obtain Tier II information about stored chemicals
Water utilities should assess treatment system capabilities to remove potential hazardous chemicals
Public health agencies should establish communication frameworks to ensure information is coordinated through one entity
American Water Works Association should disseminate lessons learned from chemical contamination incidents
American Water should establish requirements for subsidiary surface water treatment plants to develop source water protection plans

hazards.name: Stored hazardous chemicals (Crude MCHM and PPH, stripped) in aboveground storage tank
Corrosive environment causing tank material degradation
Proximity of chemical storage facility to drinking water source

threats.name: Pitting corrosion of tank floor interior surface
Deficient communication of chemical composition and quantity

consequences.name: Public health exposure and medical treatment requirements
Business, school, and public office closures
Public distrust of drinking water safety
Delayed and unclear public health communication

controls.name: Tank inspection and maintenance program
controls.barrier_role: Identify and address internal corrosion before failure
controls.lod_basis: Early detection of corrosion prevents tank failure

supporting_text: CSB found no documentation of prior inspections or maintenance conducted by Freedom or the prior facility owner, Etowah River Terminal (ERT), which would have identified and addressed internal corrosion in tank 396
Such inspections and maintenance could have identified and addressed the interior corrosion and holes in tank 396

controls.name: Secondary containment structure maintenance
controls.barrier_role: Contain leaked chemicals and prevent migration to environment
controls.lod_basis: Secondary containment prevents environmental release when primary tank fails

supporting_text: The secondary containment or dike wall, originally designed to control leaks, had cracks and holes from disrepair that allowed the mixture, containing Crude MCHM and PPH, stripped, to escape the containment
Freedom was aware of the deteriorated secondary containment wall but did not repair it prior to the incident

controls.name: Leak detection system
controls.barrier_role: Detect tank leaks and provide immediate notification
controls.lod_basis: Early detection enables rapid response to minimize release volume

supporting_text: Freedom did not have any leak prevention or leak detection system in place to immediately provide notification of tank leaks

controls.name: Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC) plan implementation
controls.barrier_role: Prevent spills through planning and operational controls
controls.lod_basis: SPCC plans establish procedures to prevent and control spills

supporting_text: CSB found no evidence that Freedom or ERT implemented a Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan or Groundwater Protection Plan

controls.name: Groundwater Protection Rule compliance
controls.barrier_role: Protect groundwater from contamination through secondary containment and monitoring
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory requirement to maintain secondary containment and groundwater protection

supporting_text: Freedom was required to maintain adequate secondary containment under the West Virginia/National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) General Water Pollution Control Permit's Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan and the Groundwater Protection Rule

controls.name: Regulatory inspection and enforcement
controls.barrier_role: Verify compliance with tank and containment requirements through inspections
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory oversight ensures facilities maintain required standards

supporting_text: WVDEP did not inspect the site for compliance with these programs due to resource constraints

controls.name: Water treatment process
controls.barrier_role: Remove chemical contaminants from drinking water
controls.lod_basis: Water treatment is the primary barrier to prevent contaminated water reaching consumers

supporting_text: The water treatment process was not capable of fully treating and removing the chemicals
WVAW's water treatment and filtration methods were unable to treat and remove all of the chemical mixture in its water treatment process

controls.name: Do Not Use order issuance
controls.barrier_role: Prevent public consumption of contaminated water
controls.lod_basis: Immediate notification prevents exposure when treatment fails

supporting_text: a 'Do Not Use' order was issued less than two hours after WVAW detected odors in the treated water intended for distribution
That evening, WVAW issued a Do Not Use (DNU) order for 93,000 customer accounts (approximately 300,000 residents) across portions of nine counties

controls.name: Source water protection planning
controls.barrier_role: Identify and assess contamination risks to drinking water sources
controls.lod_basis: Source water assessment enables proactive risk management

supporting_text: Source water protection efforts vary by state, and as a result, surface water treatment plants across the United States are subject to different requirements to protect drinking water sources

controls.name: Public health communication and risk assessment
controls.barrier_role: Communicate accurate health risk information to public and emergency responders
controls.lod_basis: Clear communication enables informed decision-making and reduces panic

supporting_text: Freedom's communication to the public, state and federal agencies, WVAW and first responders regarding the chemicals and quantity of chemicals involved in the leak was deficient

Freedom failed to immediately communicate information about all the chemicals present inside tank 396 and did not inform the public that the second chemical, a mixture of polyglycol ethers, (PPH, stripped), was also present in tank 396 until 12 days after leak discovery

residents in the Charleston area were given unclear and conflicting announcements because of the changing information from Freedom and government agencies, which increased public uncertainty about the safety of the drinking water

controls.name: Chemical information coordination with water utilities
controls.barrier_role: Ensure water utilities have immediate access to chemical composition and toxicological data
controls.lod_basis: Accurate chemical information enables appropriate treatment and response decisions

supporting_text: Freedom instead provided the SDS for Crude MCHM to WVAW and emergency responders after the spill
The DNU order was not issued immediately because WVAW was mistakenly informed that Crude MCHM was a flocculant, rather than a frothing agent, and that only 1,000 gallons was released

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
